childbirth.title: Emergency Childbirth
childbirth.description: Baby coming before you can reach a hospital

steps.text: Stay calm and reassure the mother. Panic makes everything worse.
steps.note: Your calm voice is medicine right now.

steps.text: Wash your hands thoroughly with soap and water for at least 30 seconds.
steps.note: If no soap: hand sanitizer or clean cloth.

steps.text: Lay the mother on her back with knees bent, or on her side if more comfortable.
steps.note: Use clean cloths, a mat, or a folded wrapper under her.

steps.text: Do NOT pull the baby — let the baby come naturally with each contraction.
steps.note: Pulling can injure the baby's neck.

steps.text: As the head appears, gently support it with both hands. Do not push it back.
steps.note: If the bag of water hasn't broken, peel it back from the baby's face.

steps.text: Check if the cord is around the neck. If yes, gently slip it over the head.
steps.note: Do this quickly but gently.

steps.text: After the head is out, the body usually follows in 1-2 more pushes. Support the body.
steps.note: The baby will be slippery — hold firmly but gently.

steps.text: Hold the baby slightly head-down to clear the airway. Wipe nose and mouth with a clean cloth.
steps.note: The baby should cry within 30 seconds.

steps.text: If baby doesn't cry: rub the back firmly, or flick the soles of the feet.
steps.note: If still no breathing after 1 minute, begin infant CPR.

steps.text: Keep the baby warm — skin-to-skin on the mother's chest is best. Cover both with a blanket.
steps.note: Newborns lose heat fast.

steps.text: Do NOT cut the cord until it stops pulsing (5-10 minutes), or leave it attached.
steps.note: If you must cut: tie firmly 3cm and 5cm from baby's belly, cut between ties with clean blade.

steps.text: The placenta will come 5-30 minutes after birth. Let the mother push it out naturally.
steps.note: Do NOT pull the cord to speed this up.

steps.text: If heavy bleeding after delivery: massage the lower abdomen firmly in circular motions.
steps.note: The uterus should feel hard like a grapefruit.

steps.text: Begin breastfeeding as soon as possible — it helps stop bleeding.
steps.note: Breastfeeding causes natural contractions.

steps.text: Get to a health facility as soon as possible, even if birth went well.
steps.note: Both mother and baby need professional evaluation.

steps.text: Put on gloves or use a plastic bag to protect yourself from bloodborne illness.
steps.note: If nothing available, use multiple layers of cloth.

steps.text: Apply firm, direct pressure to the wound with the cleanest cloth available.
steps.note: Use your palm, not just fingers.

steps.text: Press HARD and do not lift the cloth to check. Hold for at least 10-15 minutes.
steps.note: Checking resets the clotting process.

steps.text: If blood soaks through, add MORE cloth on top — do not remove the first layer.
steps.note: Removing disrupts the clot forming underneath.

steps.text: For limb wounds: if bleeding won't stop, apply a tourniquet 5-7cm above the wound.
steps.note: Use cloth, a belt, or a strip of clothing tied tightly.

steps.text: Write the time of tourniquet application on the person's skin.
steps.note: Tell medical staff when they arrive.

steps.text: Lay the person flat. Raise their legs 30cm if no head, neck, or spine injury suspected.
steps.note: This keeps blood flowing to vital organs.

steps.text: Keep the person warm with a blanket. Blood loss causes rapid chilling.
steps.note: Hypothermia makes bleeding worse.

steps.text: Talk to them. Keep them calm and awake if possible.
steps.note: Ask their name, where they are.

steps.text: Get to emergency care immediately. Do not remove pressure during transport.
steps.note: Blood loss is time-critical.

cpr_adult.title: CPR — Adult
cpr_adult.description: Person is unconscious and not breathing normally

steps.text: Check the scene is safe. Tap shoulders firmly and shout: 'Are you okay?'
steps.note: No response and no normal breathing = begin CPR.

steps.text: Call for help loudly. Point at a specific person: 'YOU — call emergency services now!'
steps.note: Vague calls to a crowd often go unanswered.

steps.text: Lay the person on their back on a firm flat surface.
steps.note: A soft mattress reduces CPR effectiveness.

steps.text: Kneel beside their chest. Place the heel of one hand on the centre of their chest (lower half of breastbone).
steps.note: Find the centre — between the nipples.

steps.text: Place your second hand on top, interlace fingers. Keep fingers raised off the chest.
steps.note: Only the heel of your bottom hand should touch.

steps.text: Keep arms straight. Press down 5-6cm HARD and FAST. Release completely between compressions.
steps.note: Full release lets the heart refill.

steps.text: Aim for 100-120 compressions per minute — the beat of 'Stayin' Alive' by Bee Gees.
steps.note: Count aloud: 1-2-3-4-5...

steps.text: After 30 compressions: tilt head back, lift chin. Pinch nose. Give 2 rescue breaths (1 second each).
steps.note: Skip rescue breaths if untrained — hands-only CPR is still effective.

steps.text: Continue 30:2 cycles without stopping until: person breathes, trained help arrives, or you are exhausted.
steps.note: Switch with another person every 2 minutes if possible.

steps.text: If an AED (defibrillator) is available: turn it on and follow its voice instructions immediately.
steps.note: AEDs are designed for untrained users. Use it.

steps.text: Remove the person from the heat source. If clothing is on fire: STOP, DROP, ROLL.
steps.note: Remove smouldering clothing unless stuck to skin.

steps.text: Cool the burn under cool (not cold, not ice) running water for 20 minutes.
steps.note: This is the single most important step. Start within 3 hours.

steps.text: Remove jewellery, watches, and tight items near the burn before swelling starts.
steps.note: Do this gently and quickly.

steps.text: Cover loosely with a clean non-fluffy material — cling film, a clean plastic bag, or damp cloth.
steps.note: Do NOT use fluffy cotton or adhesive bandages.

steps.text: Do NOT pop blisters. Do NOT apply toothpaste, butter, or traditional remedies to burns.
steps.note: These trap heat and cause infection.

steps.text: For chemical burns: flush with large amounts of water for 20+ minutes. Remove contaminated clothing.
steps.note: Protect yourself from the chemical.

steps.text: Give pain relief if available (paracetamol/panadol). Keep the person warm.
steps.note: Burns cause rapid heat loss.

steps.text: Seek emergency care for: burns on face/hands/genitals, burns larger than palm, in children/elderly, all electrical burns.
steps.note: When in doubt, go to a facility.

steps.text: Move away from the snake. Do NOT try to catch or kill it — more bites happen this way.
steps.note: A photo of the snake helps doctors — only if safe.

steps.text: Keep the person calm and as still as possible. Movement spreads venom faster.
steps.note: Carry the person if they must move.

steps.text: Remove rings, watches, tight clothing from the bitten limb before swelling starts.
steps.note: Swelling can be severe.

steps.text: Immobilize the bitten limb at or below heart level. Splint if possible (like a broken bone).
steps.note: Keep still. Immobilization is the #1 first aid.

steps.text: Mark the edge of any swelling with a pen and write the time. Check every 15 minutes.
steps.note: This helps doctors track how fast venom is spreading.

steps.text: Get to the nearest hospital with antivenom as fast as possible.
steps.note: Antivenom is the only treatment. Time matters.

steps.text: Do NOT: cut and suck the wound, apply tourniquet, apply ice, give alcohol.
steps.note: These worsen outcomes and waste time.

steps.text: If person stops breathing, begin CPR.
steps.note: Some neurotoxic snakes cause respiratory paralysis.

malaria.title: Malaria
malaria.description: High fever with chills in malaria-endemic area

steps.text: Check temperature. Malaria fever is typically 38.5°C (101.3°F) or higher with shivering.
steps.note: Fever may come in cycles: every 48 hours (P. vivax) or daily (P. falciparum).

steps.text: Give paracetamol/panadol to reduce fever. Follow package dosing by weight.
steps.note: Do NOT give aspirin to children under 16.

steps.text: Keep the person hydrated — oral rehydration salts (ORS) or clean water with a pinch of salt and sugar.
steps.note: High fever causes dangerous dehydration.

steps.text: Watch for danger signs: convulsions, unconsciousness, severe vomiting, inability to drink, very fast breathing.
steps.note: Any of these = go to emergency immediately.

steps.text: Get a malaria rapid diagnostic test (RDT) or blood smear at the nearest clinic as soon as possible.
steps.note: Artemisinin-based treatment (ACT) must be prescribed — do not self-treat without diagnosis.

steps.text: In children under 5: malaria can turn severe very fast. Do not wait at home if worried.
steps.note: Severe malaria in children can kill within hours.

steps.text: Complete the full course of any prescribed antimalarials, even if feeling better.
steps.note: Stopping early causes relapse and resistance.

cholera.title: Cholera / Severe Diarrhoea
cholera.description: Sudden severe watery diarrhoea and vomiting

steps.text: Start oral rehydration therapy (ORT) immediately. Every trip to the toilet = a cup of ORS.
steps.note: ORS = 1 litre clean water + 6 teaspoons sugar + ½ teaspoon salt.

steps.text: Keep drinking fluids constantly — even if vomiting, small sips every few minutes help.
steps.note: Rice water, coconut water, or diluted juice also work.

steps.text: Danger signs: sunken eyes, dry mouth, no urination for 6+ hours, extreme weakness, confusion.
steps.note: These mean severe dehydration — go to emergency now.

steps.text: In children: use a syringe or spoon to give ORS 5mL every 2-3 minutes if vomiting.
steps.note: Continue breastfeeding infants throughout.

steps.text: Wash hands with soap after every toilet visit and before food. Use separate toilet if possible.
steps.note: Cholera spreads extremely fast through faecal contamination.

steps.text: Do not share food, water, or utensils with the sick person.
steps.note: Boil all water and cook all food thoroughly.

steps.text: Get to a health facility for IV fluids and antibiotics if vomiting prevents oral hydration.
steps.note: Cholera kills through dehydration — IV fluids can save life quickly.

steps.text: Watch for: persistent fever (rising each day for 3-5 days), headache, stomach pain, constipation or diarrhoea.
steps.note: Typhoid fever builds slowly — unlike malaria which spikes.

steps.text: Give paracetamol for fever. Keep the person well hydrated with clean water and ORS.
steps.note: Do NOT use ibuprofen or aspirin — risk of bleeding.

steps.text: Ensure strict hygiene — handwashing, clean water, separate utensils.
steps.note: Typhoid spreads through contaminated food and water.

steps.text: Get to a clinic for a Widal test or blood culture to confirm typhoid.
steps.note: Antibiotics (chloramphenicol, ciprofloxacin, azithromycin) are essential — self-treatment is risky.

steps.text: Danger signs: intestinal bleeding (black or bloody stool), hole in gut (sudden severe abdominal pain), confusion.
steps.note: These are emergencies — go immediately.

steps.text: Complete the FULL antibiotic course prescribed. Typhoid relapse is common with incomplete treatment.
steps.note: Drug-resistant typhoid is increasing — only a doctor should prescribe.

meningitis.title: Meningitis
meningitis.description: Stiff neck, severe headache, fever — possible meningitis

steps.text: Check for the classic triad: fever + stiff neck (can't touch chin to chest) + severe headache.
steps.note: Any 2 of these 3 = treat as meningitis until proven otherwise.

steps.text: Check for rash: small red/purple spots that don't fade when you press a glass against them = meningococcal emergency.
steps.note: This rash means bacteria in bloodstream — minutes matter.

steps.text: Get to emergency care IMMEDIATELY. Do not wait to see if it improves.
steps.note: Meningitis can kill in 24 hours and cause deafness/disability quickly.

steps.text: In babies: bulging fontanelle (soft spot on top of head), high-pitched cry, refusing to feed, abnormal drowsiness.
steps.note: Classic stiff neck may be absent in infants.

steps.text: Keep the person comfortable, in a darkened quiet room. Light and noise worsen symptoms.
steps.note: Photophobia (light sensitivity) is a key sign.

steps.text: Do not give food or drink if they may need emergency surgery or are vomiting.
steps.note: IV access and urgent antibiotics are the only effective treatment.

choking.title: Choking
choking.description: Person cannot breathe due to blocked airway

steps.text: Ask: 'Are you choking?' If they can cough, speak, or breathe — encourage coughing. Do not intervene yet.
steps.note: Effective coughing is more powerful than any first aid.

steps.text: If they CANNOT cough, speak, or breathe: stand behind them, lean them forward.
steps.note: Lean them so their head is lower than their chest.

steps.text: Give 5 firm back blows between shoulder blades with the heel of your hand.
steps.note: Hard and fast. Each blow should be separate.

steps.text: If not cleared: give 5 abdominal thrusts (Heimlich). Stand behind, wrap arms around waist, make a fist above navel, pull sharply upward-inward.
steps.note: For pregnant women or obese: use chest thrusts instead.

steps.text: Alternate 5 back blows and 5 abdominal thrusts until object clears or person goes unconscious.
steps.note: Be forceful — lives depend on it.

steps.text: If person goes unconscious: lower them to the ground, call for help, start CPR — begin with 30 compressions.
steps.note: Before rescue breaths: look in mouth and remove any visible object.

steps.text: For infants under 1 year: use 5 back blows + 5 chest thrusts (NOT abdominal thrusts). Hold face-down across your forearm.
steps.note: Never do blind finger sweeps in infants.

start.text: What is most urgently concerning right now?

options.label: Someone is unconscious or not breathing
I have mild symptoms, want to understand them

unconscious.title: Potential emergency — act immediately
unconscious.advice: Check for breathing. If not breathing normally, begin CPR. Send someone to call emergency services right now.

fever_illness.text: How high is the fever and how long has it lasted?

options.label: Very high (39°C+) and started suddenly with shivering
Moderate, building over several days with stomach pain
High fever with severe headache and stiff neck
Fever with vomiting and severe watery diarrhoea

malaria_path.title: Possible Malaria
malaria_path.advice: Symptoms match malaria pattern — common in this region. Get to a clinic for rapid test (RDT) today. Give paracetamol for fever and keep hydrated while arranging transport.

typhoid_path.title: Possible Typhoid Fever
typhoid_path.advice: Gradual fever building over days with abdominal symptoms suggests typhoid. Strict hygiene is essential. See a doctor for testing and prescribed antibiotics.

meningitis_path.title: Possible Meningitis — Go immediately
meningitis_path.advice: Fever + headache + stiff neck is a medical emergency. Get to the nearest hospital NOW. Every hour matters with meningitis.

cholera_path.title: Possible Cholera / Severe Gastroenteritis
cholera_path.advice: Start oral rehydration immediately (ORS or clean water + sugar + salt). Go to a health facility if vomiting prevents drinking or if signs of severe dehydration appear.
